raw_case:
Bacillus megaterium infection presenting as pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, a case report


Background

Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP) is a special clinical presentation mostly associated with autoimmune disorders. Here we report a rare case of PAP secondary to infection of Bacillus megaterium.

Case presentation

A 58-year-old woman presented with intermittent cough and dyspnea for half a year. Chest CT scan showed “crazy paving” pattern. B. megaterium was identified by percutaneous CT-guided needle biopsy. She continuously received antimicrobial treatment since the diagnosis and follow-up examination suggested great improvement.

Conclusions

To our knowledge, this is the first case of B. megaterium infection presented with PAP pattern in healthy individuals. Attention should be paid on the secondary causes including rare pathogen infection when patients presented with PAP syndrome.

Supplementary Information

The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12879-024-09713-2.

Background

Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis (PAP) is a diffuse lung disease characterized by the “crazy paving” pattern on chest CT scan and accumulation of lipoproteinaceous material in the alveoli. While in most cases, PAP is associated with autoantibodies against granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), PAP could occur secondary to infection, immune deficiency or other environmental factors. Bacillus megaterium is a Gram-positive, aerobic, spore-forming, and rod-shaped bacterium widely distributed in the environment. It has been used in agriculture due to its ability to decompose organic phosphorus compounds. Generally, B. megaterium is known to be non-pathogenic or, at least, of very low virulence. Here we describe a rare case of secondary PAP caused by B. megaterium infection.

Case presentation

A 58-year-old female patient was referred with intermittent cough and shortness of breath presented half a year ago. She had a history of hypertension and coronary artery disease. She sought medical attention at local hospital, where routine blood tests and sputum pathogen screening were all negative. Chest CT showed multifocal patchy ground-glass opacities in both lungs and bilateral pleural thickening. She went through twice bronchoscopy examination. Bronchoalveolar Lavage Fluid (BALF) showed opaque and milky-white appearance. BALF-mNGS suggested infections with Haemophilus influenzae (sequence count 12559) and Klebsiella oxytoca (sequence count 1287), with no evidence of fungal or virus infection. She was subsequently treated with Methylprednisolone for a week and Cefoperazone-Sulbactam plus Voriconazole for half a month, yet no significant improvement was observed.On examination, the patient was afebrile, and presented with the following vital signs: temperature, 36 °C; heart rate, 96 beats/min; respirations, 18 breaths/min; BP, 124/68 mmHg; and oxygen saturation, 94% on room air. Bilateral end-expiratory wheezes and crackles were present on lung auscultation at lower lung fields. The remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.The routine laboratory tests showed elevated inflammatory markers, including a CRP level of 101.87 mg/L and ESR level of 74 ml/h. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) reached 541U/L and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) was 4.38 mmol/L. Two tumor markers were slightly elevated (CEA 30.8 ng/mL and Cyfra21-1 14 ng/mL, respectively). Tests for serum antibodies revealed positive antinuclear antibody (titer 1:80) and negative anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies to myeloperoxidase and proteinase-3. Arterial blood gas indicated lower PaO2 of 65 mmHg and higher alveolar-arterial gradient of 41.3 mmHg. Other routine blood tests including complete blood count, biochemistry, urinalysis, HIV test, C3 and C4 levels were normal. Pulmonary function tests (PFT) demonstrated restrictive ventilatory impairment and severe diffusion limitation with total lung capacity 56% predicted and diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide 35% predicted. High resolution chest CT showed “crazy-paving” pattern symmetrically in bilateral lung fields that consisted of septal line thickening superimposed on areas of ground glass opacity. Localized consolidation could be found on the left upper lobe (Fig. 1).Due to the atypical presence of the left upper lobe, percutaneous CT-guided needle biopsy was performed for definitive diagnosis. The histologic result suggested granular eosinophilic material and macrophages filled in airspaces. Special staining suggested weakly positive Periodic acid-Schiff-diastase stain and Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining, and negative mucicarmine mucin stain (Fig. 2). No granulomas were observed. Bacterial culture finally indicated the presence of B. megaterium (bacterial count 2 +).Based on her CT scan, histologic result and bacterial culture, the diagnosis of PAP was confirmed. The patient was treated with clindamycin intravenously for eight days and switched to amoxicillin-clavulanate orally. GM-CSF antibody level was further tested and reached borderline significant (4.5 μg/ml, > 4 μg/ml is considered as autoimmune PAP). Considering the relatively low level of GM-CSF antibody and acceptable oxygen saturation, GM-CSF therapy and Whole-lung lavage (WLL) were postponed. The patient has been taken amoxicillin-clavulanate 0.375 g ter in die since then and her situation remained stable. The follow-up chest CT a month later showed significant improvement (Fig. 3).

Discussion and conclusions

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first adult PAP secondary to B. megaterium infection described in the medical literature. Previous literature has reported few cases of B. megaterium infection in certain sites including the cornea, soft tissue of the skin, brain, and pleura [1]. Most occurred in immunocompromised hosts with triggers like medical surgery or trauma. All patients could be completely cured with properly prolonged antimicrobial treatment.PAP is a diffuse lung disease characterized by the accumulation of lipoproteinaceous material in the alveoli and dysfunction of alveolar macrophages. It occurs in about 7 people per million in the general population. PAP can be classified into different types according to the pathogenetic mechanism. Primary PAP is caused by the disruption of GM-CSF signaling and could be further divided into autoimmune and hereditary form. Secondary PAP results from various underlying conditions including malignancies and infections. Congenital PAP is caused by mutations in genes involved in surfactant production.Common symptoms of PAP include shortness of breath, cough and chest pain, while up to a third of patients can be asymptomatic. Crackles might be present but are not universal. Antibodies to GM-CSF can be identified in almost all autoimmune PAP, both in the serum and BALF. Other potential serological markers including LDH, CEA, Cyfra21-1 have been investigated as surrogates of disease severity and progression, but clinical availability, sensitivity and specificity remain unknown. PFT typically reveal restrictive impairment and reduction of diffusing capacity. High resolution CT scan usually presents “crazy-paving” pattern characterized by diffuse, ground-glass opacities with septal thickening and subpleural sparing. BALF demonstrates opaque, milky appearance. Histologic evaluation shows granular eosinophilic material filling alveolar spaces that is positive for PAS staining. The diagnosis of PAP is made by specific CT scan, BALF and histologic patterns. For the treatment of PAP, besides oxygen therapy and smoking cessation, some patients with mild symptoms may experience spontaneous remission. Inhaled GM-CSF therapy was particularly aimed at autoimmune PAP. Treatment of underlying disorders should be considered in patients with secondary causes. Whole-lung lavage (WLL) is the cornerstone of PAP therapy when patients have severe hypoxemia or intrapulmonary shunt and no remission was achieved by treatments mentioned above [2, 3].In the setting of chronic infection, treatment of infection alone may improve patients’ condition. Patients with PAP are also at increased risk of secondary infection with common and opportunistic pathogens. Due to the marginally elevated GM-CSF antibody, this could be a case of autoimmune PAP with secondary infection. A previous study showed the commonly reported opportunistic infections in patients with PAP included nocardiosis, mycobacteria and fungi [4]. B. megaterium infection was seldom reported. Antimicrobial or antifungal therapy together with WLL should be considered. In either case, once infection is confirmed, antimicrobial therapy is recommended and improvement could be expected.In conclusion, PAP should be considered in apparently immunocompetent patients who present with an opportunistic infection like B. megaterium. PAP can be a focal histologic finding, which may not always be detected on BAL or transbronchial biopsy. Apart from mNGS, traditional method like percutaneous needle biopsy or surgical biopsies should not be neglected.

Supplementary Information


Supplementary material 1: Peer reviews.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 58-year-old female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Intermittent cough and shortness of breath for half a year.
- **History of Present Illness:** Symptoms persisted for six months. Initial tests including blood tests and sputum pathogen screenings were negative. Partial improvement was achieved with methylprednisolone and antibiotics (Cefoperazone-Sulbactam plus Voriconazole). Bronchoscopy revealed white, milky bronchoalveolar lavage fluid but no improvement in clinical condition.
- **Past Medical History:** Hypertension and coronary artery disease.
- **Personal History:** None mentioned.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Afebrile, vital signs within normal limits but showed oxygen saturation of 94% on room air. Crackles and wheezes found bilaterally in lower lung fields.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Blood test results: Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP: 101.87 mg/L, ESR: 74 ml/h), LDH: 541 U/L, elevated LDL-C, tumor markers CEA (30.8 ng/mL) and Cyfra21-1 (14 ng/mL). Positive antinuclear antibody (titer 1:80).
  - Pulmonary function tests: Restrictively impaired with reduced diffusion capacity.
  - Arterial blood gas: Lower PaO2 (65 mmHg) and higher alveolar-arterial gradient (41.3 mmHg).
  - Chest CT: "Crazy-paving" pattern indicating diffuse ground glass opacities with septal thickening.
  - Biopsy: Percutaneous CT-guided biopsy showed granular eosinophilic material filling alveolar spaces (weakly PAS-positive). Bacterial culture confirmed Bacillus megaterium.
  - Further testing for GM-CSF antibodies was borderline significant (4.5 μg/mL; normal threshold >4 μg/mL).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnoses:**
   - **Autoimmune PAP:** Suggested by the “crazy-paving” pattern, milky BALF, PAS-positive histology, marginally elevated GM-CSF antibody level.
   - **Infectious PAP (secondary to opportunistic infection):**
     - Supported by bacterial culture identifying Bacillus megaterium and absence of fungal or viral pathogens.
     - Opportunistic infections like nocardiosis, mycobacteria, or other pathogens.
   - **Malignancy:** Slightly elevated tumor markers (CEA and Cyfra21-1), but no evidence of malignancy on imaging or histology.
2. **Analysis and Exclusion:**
   - **Autoimmune PAP:** GM-CSF antibody was borderline significant but not conclusive. Histologic findings and clinical presentation fit better with an infectious etiology.
   - **Non-opportunistic infection or typical pathogens:** Ruled out based on negative sputum screens and no significant clinical improvement initially with broad-spectrum antibiotics (Cefoperazone-Sulbactam, Voriconazole).
   - **Neoplasm:** Excluded due to lack of evidence to support malignancy on imaging and biopsy.
   - **Infectious PAP secondary to Bacillus megaterium:** Confirmed due to positive biopsy bacterial culture, classic PAP radiologic patterns, histology, and clinical improvement following appropriate antimicrobial treatment (clindamycin and amoxicillin-clavulanate).
3. **Final Determination:** Infectious PAP secondary to Bacillus megaterium was established as the most likely cause, supported by histologic and microbiologic findings alongside clinical outcomes.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis (PAP) secondary to Bacillus megaterium infection.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis (PAP) secondary to Bacillus megaterium infection.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Analyze the chief complaint and history of present illness. The patient presents with chronic respiratory symptoms (intermittent cough and shortness of breath) for six months. Initial investigations were negative, but there was a partial response to corticosteroids and broad-spectrum antibiotics, suggesting an inflammatory or infectious process, although not fully resolving. The milky bronchoalveolar lavage fluid is an important atypical finding.

<step 2> Consider the past medical history. Hypertension and coronary artery disease are present, but not directly related to the current respiratory illness. They are relevant comorbidities to consider in overall patient management.

<step 3> Evaluate the physical examination findings. The patient is afebrile, but has mild hypoxemia (SpO2 94% on room air) and bilateral crackles and wheezes in the lower lung fields, indicating lung parenchymal disease, likely involving both alveoli and small airways.

<step 4> Examine the blood test results. Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, LDH) confirm systemic inflammation. Elevated tumor markers (CEA, Cyfra21-1) are concerning but can be elevated in inflammatory conditions and are not specific for malignancy in this context without further evidence. The positive ANA (1:80) suggests a possible autoimmune component, although the titer is low and can be non-specific. Elevated LDL-C is related to past medical history (CAD).

<step 5> Analyze the pulmonary function tests and arterial blood gas results. Restrictive impairment with reduced diffusion capacity and hypoxemia with an increased alveolar-arterial gradient are consistent with interstitial lung disease or alveolar filling processes that impair gas exchange.

<step 6> Interpret the chest CT findings. The "crazy-paving" pattern (diffuse ground glass opacities with septal thickening) is a key finding, highly suggestive of conditions like pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP), Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP), acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), and certain interstitial lung diseases. In the context of chronic symptoms and other findings, PAP becomes a strong contender.

<step 7> Assess the biopsy results. The finding of granular eosinophilic material filling alveolar spaces (weakly PAS-positive) is very significant and strongly points towards Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis (PAP). The weak PAS positivity is also consistent with PAP.

<step 8> Consider the bacterial culture result. *Bacillus megaterium* is identified. While *Bacillus* species are generally considered contaminants, they can rarely cause infection, particularly in immunocompromised individuals. However, given the overall clinical and pathological picture, it's less likely to be the primary cause of this patient's condition. It might represent colonization or a secondary infection in the context of underlying lung disease.

<step 9> Evaluate the GM-CSF antibody test result. The borderline significant GM-CSF antibody level (4.5 μg/mL; normal threshold >4 μg/mL) is highly suggestive of autoimmune pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (aPAP).  aPAP is the most common form of PAP and is characterized by the presence of GM-CSF autoantibodies.  While borderline, in the context of all other findings, this strengthens the diagnosis of aPAP.

<step 10> Synthesize all findings to arrive at the final diagnosis. Combining the chronic respiratory symptoms, "crazy-paving" pattern on CT, milky bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, granular eosinophilic material in alveolar spaces on biopsy (weakly PAS-positive), and borderline positive GM-CSF antibodies, the most likely diagnosis is **Autoimmune Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis (aPAP)**.  While other differential diagnoses were considered, the constellation of findings strongly points towards aPAP as the definitive diagnosis. The partial response to steroids could be due to the inflammatory component of PAP, although PAP is primarily a disease of surfactant accumulation.

### Answer:
Autoimmune Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis
